The Management commented that the delay in the implementation was said to be caused by re-prioritization of the Local Chief Executive to effectively push through with other projects and setting aside another.  Some projects require a thorough review by Sangguniang Bayan (SB) prior to implementation, was said to be causing additional lead time for pending projects.

We recommended that Members of the Municipal Local School Board:
prepare a balanced budget with conscious and concerted efforts of observing compliance with the legal and regulatory requirements on SEF budgeting, allocation and utilization and stop the practice of accommodating expenditures for activities for which the DepEd has the direct mandate to execute;
request for inputs from the Deped official/School Division Superintendent in the prioritization of SEF projects and expenditures in accordance with the new Joint Circular No. 1, s.2017 published by DepEd, DILG and DBM with effectivity date of January 19, 2017; and
maximize the utilization of SEF to attain its objectives, monitor the implementation of the Project Program Activities (PPA’s), and take appropriate actions such as realignment or reprograming, if necessary.
The officials concerned justified that the officials and materials were used by the Local School Board while the purchase of one unit laptop is intended for the preparation, printing of teachers’ guides, forms and pamphlets which were included under Section 1 of RA 5447. They however, acknowledged the Audit Team’s observation and assured compliance with the regulation in the ensuing budget year.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) coordinate efforts and implement continuously the five basic environmental laws on preserving and protecting the environment expressly stated under R.A. No. 9003; and (b) instruct the Municipal Solid Waste Division to monitor the establishment and operations of barangay MRFs and provide the necessary support in implementation of waste segregation at source and control measures on waste management at the barangay level.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the GFPS to properly plan and regularly monitor the implementation of GAD projects and activities and prioritize those activities that can be undertaken for the budget year that would address the identified GAD issues.

We recommended that the (a) Acting Municipal Treasurer continue to promptly remit withheld GSIS contributions/premiums/loan amortizations from employees compensations within the prescribed period and (b) the Municipal Accountant reconcile the Due to GSIS account records with the GSIS to determine any errors in recording thereof.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant (a) continue to comply with its duty to withhold taxes due on compensation and all money payments to suppliers, contractors and other creditors and promptly remit the same to the Bureau of Internal Revenue in accordance with Revenue Memorandum Circular No. 23-2007 and BIR Tax Revenue Regulation No. 10-2008.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) cause the immediate settlement of all outstanding audit suspensions and disallowances; and (b) require the responsible officials and employees/persons liable to refund their disallowance otherwise, cause the withholding of their salary and other emoluments due to them until fully settled.

We recommended that the Head of the Agency (a) instruct the Municipal Accountant to (i) strictly comply with the regulation on the rendition of accounts and financial reports for the timely verification and audit of accounts; and (b) make proper representation with the depository banks of the Municipality to send the bank statements within the prescribed timeframe of five days after the end of each month, in order to give ample time for the preparation of the BRS by the Municipal Accounting personnel for submission of Bank Reconciliation Statements to COA. Alternatively, the Municipality may enroll its depository accounts with the “We Access” facility of the LBP which allows the on-line and almost real time generation of statement of depository accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer  (a)  in coordination with the heads of Offices, (i) conduct a thorough review and physical count of the property and equipment of the Municipality in order to present a realistic balance of property accounts and make a fair presentation of accounts in the financial statements;  (ii) undertake efforts to locate the TCT of the 32 properties supported with Tax Declaration to provide basis for proper recognition of these properties in the books of accounts; and (b) renew the registration of all serviceable motor vehicles of the Municipality with LTO pursuant to Batas Pambansa Bilang 74, to avoid additional expenses from payment of damages to third person liabilities and penalties in case of vehicular accidents.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the: (a) Head of the Municipal Agriculture Office to: (i) provide the Audit Team a certified true copy of the MOA executed with the DA for further verification and evaluation; (ii) cause the cancellation of the deed of donation executed in favor of the two farmers’ associations that were actually denied of the STWs; (iii) execute another deed of donation for the two STWs given to the barangay council; and (iv) submit a written explanation on the change of STWs beneficiaries; and (b) Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and different LGU units to strictly comply with the provisions of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184, GPPB guidelines and the LGU-NGAS Manual.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) convene the MDC as frequently as necessary to facilitate the preparation of the CDP of the Municipality consistent with the guidelines in its preparation to prevent the wastage of funds and ensure that PPAs implemented under the 20% DF are truly the inputs from the CDP towards the realization of the Municipality’s development targets; (b) direct the Municipal Engineer to aim for the full utilization and implementation of the identified development projects under the 20% DF; and (c) advise the MDC to perform due diligence in the identification and prioritization of the development projects along with its available budget to ensure the most ideal funding source of identified high-impact or capital expenditure projects for optimum attainment of the intent of the 20% DF.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive thru the Head of the LDRRMO to (a) strictly comply with the provisions of RA No. 10121; (b) institute the functional organization of the ICS, the required training and resource management which include the personnel, teams, equipment, and other facilities available during emergencies, as a perfect tool in responding to all types of hazards most particularly in an on-scene emergency situations and calamities such as flooding; (c) ensure the correctness of the monthly report on the sources and utilization of the LDRMMF and facilitate monitoring of the accomplishment vis-à-vis targeted projects for effective and efficient utilization of the Fund; (d) facilitate the transfer of unexpended balance of the LDRMF to the Trust Fund accounts.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) prioritize the implementation of programs that were identified in the SWMP to address the inefficiencies in the management of solid waste as discussed in the preceding paragraphs; (b) undertake an intensive information campaign to involve the general public in the cleanliness and beautification projects of the locality and conduct regular monitoring on the status of implementation of the PPAs relevant to solid waste management; and (c) encourage the general public’s participation through an incentive scheme like the selection of cleanest and greenest purok/household, awarding/recognition of most compliant purok/household to RA No. 9003, and the like which will help the Municipality achieve full compliance with the law on solid waste management.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) issue a supplemental order to specifically identify the members of the Technical Working Group (TWG) of the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) pursuant to Rule VI, Section 37 of the Magna Carta of Women; (b) encourage and direct the GFPS to perform its mandated tasks and to strengthen the monitoring and evaluation system to assess the effectiveness and efficiency of their gender mainstreaming efforts; (c) where feasible, cause the establishment of a GAD Office or unit under the Office of the LCE that will support and coordinate all GAD-related PPAs and concerns of the LGU GFPS. The LCE may also appoint or designate the LGU personnel to manage the GAD office and/or unit; and (d) direct the GFPS and its TWG  to (i) exert  efforts to come up with a database on sex-disaggregated data or perform gender analysis to ensure that allocation of funds on GAD-related PPAs to ensure is not based on incorrect assumption and stereotypes; (ii)  ensure that the allocated funds are maximized for the implementation of the GAD activities; (iii) conscientiously follow the prescribed template for the preparation of the GAD Plan and Budget (GPB) and GAD Accomplishment Report; and (iv) strictly comply with the pertinent provisions of issued guidelines on GAD particularly that of RA No. 9710 and PCW-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to conduct detailed review and analysis of long outstanding accounts and coordinate with GSIS for a speedy reconciliation of records/accounts; and henceforth remit all the contributions deducted due to GSIS within the prescribed period to enable the agency to use the needed funds for their operations in a timely manner.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the General Services Office or its equivalent to ensure that all insurable properties are provided with insurance coverage with the GSIS in order for it to be indemnified for the value of government properties lost through theft or any unforeseen events.

Based on the foregoing, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
formulate a policy guideline that will establish criteria, set limit, and condition in the grant of cash assistance to individuals in crisis situations;
consider the necessity of providing health services program by giving free medicines, laboratory examinations, and discounts to qualified constituents with medical condition in lieu of cash assistance; and
ensure that medical certificates are supported with hospital bill, clinical abstract and laboratory test results, to attest the medical condition being certified not only the by RHU physicians, but also by other physicians issuing medical certificates.

We recommended that management strictly enforce settlement by the persons liable of the audit disallowances within the prescribed period, pursuant to the 2009 Revised Rules of Procedures of the Commission on Audit.

We recommend that Management should (1) demand for the immediate settlement of outstanding advances; (2) strictly adhere with the governing laws, rules and regulations on the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances; and (3) to refrain from granting additional cash advances to accountable officers unless his/her previous cash advance is liquidated or a proper accounting thereof has already been made.

We recommend that the General Services Office maintains property cards for easy verification of existing properties and the conduct of the inventory taking would be lot easier too.
Require the Accountant also to prepare the adjusting entries on the misclassified properties.

We recommend that the Municipal Engineer furnish the Accountant with advance copies of the Certificate of Completion and Acceptance of projects even before payment is being processed. The Accountant shall transfer and properly record the completed and on-going projects to the appropriate agency asset in order to present fairly the value of the CIP accounts in the financial statements.

We recommend that management directs the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the reciprocal accounts and effect adjustments to reflect the correct account balances and present reliable data pursuant to Section 111(2) of Presidential Decree No. 1445.

We recommend that management prepares subsidiary ledger and/or schedules of all the accounts including the details of the items listed therein on a timely basis to establish the existence, accuracy and reliability of the recorded amounts.
We recommend that the misclassified accounts be reclassified/adjusted by the Municipal Accountant in conformity with the standard chart of accounts for fair presentation of the financial statements.

We recommend strict compliance with the aforementioned laws, rules and regulations on all government procurement. Stop the practice of reimbursements for purchase of goods and services and on account of the agency’s programs and activities. Lastly, ensure that all payments must be made by check except when the circumstances call for impracticability, difficulty and impossibility of payments through checks.

Require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to adhere to the provisions of R.A. 9184 and its Implementing Rules and Regulations. Conduct public bidding in the purchases of goods and shopping should only be resorted to in exceptional cases as provided in the IRR.
Observe the thresholds in the procurement of goods and implementation of infrastructure projects. Avoid splitting the requisition to evade the conduct of public bidding.

We recommend that Management directs the Bids and Awards Committee to strictly complies with the provision on mandatory posting of procurement opportunities of the LGU and be reminded that EO 662, as amended by EO 662-A expressly provides that non-compliance with posting obligations shall render the accountable officials liable for dereliction of duty.
We recommend strict compliance with Section 34 of the IRR-A of RA 9184 so bona fide bidder could be awarded the contract.
We recommend that management requires the suppliers, manufacturers and distributors to establish warranty deposit in accordance with Section 62.1 of Revised IRR of R.A. 9184.
We recommend strict compliance with the above issuances by submitting perfected contracts/purchase orders and inspection reports within the time frame provided by regulations.

We therefore recommend that the personnel in-charge in the segregation and submission of the accounts to be mindful of the due dates of the submission of accounts for the timely review and evaluation of such.

We recommend that management strengthen the linkage of planning and budgeting in making the budget more credible. Also, proper recording of transactions should at all times be adhered to in making the financial reports reliable.
Further, correcting entries should be made by the Municipal Accountant to correctly record the acquisition of property to its proper account.

We recommend that the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council (LDRRMC), as fund in-charge, strictly complies the procedures in the utilization of the QRF and the LDRRMF as a whole to preclude incurrence of suspension and disallowance in the future.

We recommend that Management ensure that the 20% Development Fund be optimally utilized to help achieve desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes as it is the responsibility of every local chief executive to do so and to remind management that utilizing the fund for any purpose beyond those expressly prescribed by law or public policy could be subject to sanctions provided under the Local Government Code.
Further, avoid charging ineligible expenditures to the 20% DF to preclude incurrence of future disallowance.

We recommend that Management strictly follow the fundamental principles in governing financial affairs, transactions and operations of the local government unit with emphasis on Section 305 (a) of the Local Government Code.
Further, to be cautious in disbursing funds without the support of an appropriation to preclude disallowance.

We recommend that the Local School Board, as mandated in RA 5447, properly determine the needs of the schools within its jurisdiction, so that the real purpose of the creation of the Fund could be fully achieved. Stop charging ineligible expenses to the SEF and follow strictly Section 272 of RA 7160 on the proper utilization of the fund.

We recommend that the Local Officials prioritize the allocation of budget for carrying out the programs, projects and activities in the approved Solid Waste Management Plan. Further, informed the constituents of the importance of waste management through IEC because unless its importance is acknowledged by all sectors, waste management efforts will not progress to further heights.
Furthermore, for the LGU to adopt specific revenue-generating measures to support the viability of its Solid Waste Management Plan if it lacks funds to implement it.

We recommend that LDRRMC includes in the plan only those considered disaster risk management related activities and to observed the proper utilization of the LDRRM Fund to preclude future audit disallowance.

We recommend that Management, through the local finance committee, take the necessary safety nets relative to availing credit facilities to preclude occurrence of needing to allocate a lion’s share of their Internal Revenue Allotment to repay loans leaving little funds for the delivery of essential public services.
Further, refrain from disbursing funds without the support of an appropriation to preclude disallowance.

In view of the material discrepancy of the PPE accounts and continuous
failure of the LGU to correct such deficiencies, we recommended that the Municipal
Mayor instruct the following:

1. The Inventory Team conduct complete inventory-taking of all the LGU’s
properties and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant
and Equipment to be submitted to the Auditor concerned not later than
January 31 of each year; and

2. The Municipal Accountant and the GSO/Property Officer maintain complete
and updated PPE Ledger Cards and Property Cards, respectively, to facilitate
the reconciliation of both records.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Treasurer to effect the cancellation of the stale checks and the Municipal Accountant
to make proper adjustments in the municipality’s books of accounts to fairly present
its financial statements as of December 31, 2016.

We recommended that the Municipality strictly observe provisions of Article
210 to 213 and 215 of RA 7160 and Section 3.4 of National Budget Circular No. 563
April 22, 2016. Contributions and elective officials expenses related to leagues
which are not included in the preceding articles are not government expenses and
should not be charged against the funds of the municipality. In addition, the
previously mentioned budget circular specifically disallows individual membership
fees paid to private organizations.

We also recommended that the amount of £23,500.00 be refunded by the
concerned municipal officials or employees.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly comply with the
previously cited auditing and accounting laws and rules and regulations. Submit the
disbursement voucher and supporting documents to the Auditor within the
prescribed period to avoid suspension/disallowance in audit.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal
Accountant to record expenses according to the corresponding accounts prescribed
under the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units to accurately
present the LGU’s financial statements. These statements should provide
information useful for decision making, and to demonstrate the accountability of the
entity for the resources entrusted to it, by:

(a) Providing information about the sources, allocation, and uses of financial
resources;

(b) Providing information about how the entity financed its activities and met its
cash requirements;

(¢) Providing information that is useful in evaluating the entity’s ability to finance
its activities and to meet its liabilities and commitments;

(d) Providing information about the financial condition of the entity and changes in
it; and

(e) Providing aggregate information useful in evaluating the enti
terms of service costs, efficiency, and accomplishments,

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure the efficient, effective
and optimal utilization of the 20% Development Fund which is primarily intended
to fund projects that shall contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic
development.  We further recommended that development projects to be
implemented be assessed on the impact it will have on the intended beneficiaries.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor enforce full compliance with the
provisions of Republic Act No. 9003 or “Philippine Ecological Solid Waste
Management Act of 2000” that geared towards implementation of the reduce, reuse
and recycle concept with focus on reduction of pollution and waste generation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require concerned officials to
abide by the declared policies RA 9710 or “The Magna Carta of Women” to
encourage all government instrumentalities to promote gender-responsive
governance, protect and fulfill women’s human rights, and promote women’s
economic empowerment,

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Accountant to observe strictly accounting and auditing laws, rules and regulations
in the submission of the year-end financial statements. Likewise, we recommended
that the Local Chief Exccutives apply sanctions under the existing laws, rules and
regulations previously mentioned if the circumstances do warrant its
implementation.

We recommended that the Municipality formulate its long-term development
plan and the offshoot local development investment program. The Comprehensive
Development Plan and the Local Development Investment Program are mandated
in RA 7160 for each local government unit to have a comprehensive multi-sectoral
development plan and to set a vision, sectoral goals, objectives, development
strategies and policies and translate these visions, goals and objectives into
programs, projects and activities that address the development thrusts of the LGU.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant to provide and maintain a complete set of subsidiary ledgers for special
accounts and prepare and submit financial statements as required in Section 313 of
RA 7160 and Sections 107 and 110 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume 1.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the official concern to
properly utilize the fund for the implementation of programs/projects under Local
Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund(LDRRMF) for the benefit of
constituents particularly in the affected and vulnerable areas and to refer to Section
5.1.10 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 and Section 21 of
Republic Act 10121 for the recording and reporting of LDRRMF and use of 30
percent and 70 percent calamity fund.

We also recommended the Municipal Accountant prepare subsidiary ledger
by year for prior years’ unexpended balances of the fund under the account Trust
Liability - DRRMF and furnish regularly the Office of the Auditor of the Report on
Sources and Utilization of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require immediate
implementation of all the program/projects under BuB for the benefits and
development of its constituents.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant to strictly comply with the requirements set forth in the DBM-DILG-
DSWD-NAPC Joint Memorandum Circular No. 6 and COA Cir. No. 2012-001.
Ensure that all claims against government funds be supported with all the required
documents.

Likewise, we recommended for the immediate submission of all the lacking
documents and refrain from paying disbursement vouchers with incomplete
supporting documents.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive strictly implement the
provisions of the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act No.
(R.A.) 9184 and of the Procurement Manual for Local Government Units that
regular offices under him shall be represented in the Municipal Bids and Awards
Committee.

We recommended and management agreed that the (i) LCE (a) require the Municipal Treasurer to cause the collecting officers to regularly prepare Report of Collections and Deposits at the close of each business day and remit their collections daily, particularly when collections exceed P5,000.00; (b) require the Municipal Treasurer to strictly deposit the  amount of collections on hand or in vault as prescribed; (c) instruct the collectors to issue official receipts in chronological sequence and (d) require both the Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile their records to ensure the completeness and reliability of the reports; and (ii) the Municipal Treasurer to cause the reporting of unrecorded ORs and the Accountant to cause the recording of the unrecorded collections representing  the three unreported ORs totalling P1,110.02.
observations.managementAction: Required the collector to prepare RCD and instructed the Liquidation Officer to deposit collections on the same day.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE requires the Municipal Treasurer to effect closure of cash deposits with Cooperative Bank of Aurora and subsequently transfer bank account balances with an Authorized AGDB either Land Bank of the Philippines or the Development  Bank of the Philippines as prescribed under Department of Finance DO  No. 027-05 dated December 09, 2005, DOF DO No. 001-2015 dated June 1, 2015 and DOF Department Circular 003-2015 dated August 24, 2015.
observations.managementAction: Requested  SB resolution authorizing the Municipal Mayor to effect closure of cash deposit with Cooperative Bank of Aurora and subsequently transfer with the authorized government depository bank.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) stop the practice of using funds intended for trust liabilities to other purposes; (b) apply effective cash programming to facilitate the proper handling of cash such that it is available when needed for payment of obligations and at the same time, optimize its utilization; (c)prepare Cash Flow Forecast, Cash Flow Analysis, Cash Receipts Forecast and Cash Disbursements Forecast to aid management in its cash programming; (d) adopt austerity measures on disbursements to improve the cash position of the Municipality; and (e) adopt additional revenue measures to augment the revenues specifically by considering the revision of the Local Revenue Code.
observations.managementAction: Applied effective cash programming and adopt ousterity measures on disbursement to improve the cash position of the municipality
observations.reason: The management was continuosly implementing austerity measures.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and management agreed that the Municipal Accountant (a) exert effort to trace the unreconciled difference of P534,380.51; and (b) consider the creation of a task force to do the reconciliation.
observations.managementAction: Continously exerting efforts to reconcile the accounts
observations.reason: The Accounting Office is still in the process of reconciling the accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and management agreed that the Municipal Engineer (a) avoid including the name of politician in the posting of tarpaulin for the infrastructure projects; and (b) cause the publicity of all programs/projects/activities in accordance with the requirements/provisions of COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013.

We recommended and management agreed that the Accounting Office, MGSO and MSWDO staff revisit COA Circular No. 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014 and comply with the required records, reports and registries to be prepared and maintained for DRRM Funds, cash donation and donation-in-kind to further achieve transparency and accountability in the use of disaster relief aid/donations.
observations.managementAction: The MSWDO discussed the circular with Municipal Accountant and was referred to ICO-Mun. Treasurer for the cash donations. MSWDO also discussed with MDRRMO the COA Circular.

observations.recommendation: In view of the foregoing, we reiterated our previous years’ audit recommendation that the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer determine the causes of the discrepancies between the subsidiary ledgers and the cash books for subsequent appropriate action.
observations.managementAction: The concerned Offices will exert efforts to locate the discrepancies and the reconciliation report will be submitted to the Auditor’s Office for review, once it is completed. Further, we were informed that the discrepancy may pertain to check disbursements that were recorded into a separate Cashbook which unfortunately could not be located.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the MDC ensure that the development plans of the Municipality are translated into viable programs and projects which are specifically identified and presented in the Annual Investment Plan (AIP) by describing in detail the program/project, its brief explanation, period of implementation, expense class, and cost requirement in order to measure actual performance against targeted results and strengthen budgetary planning, execution and control/monitoring.
observations.managementAction: The Chief of the Municipal Development Council agreed with our recommendations and informed us that they have already implemented these in the AIP for CY 2016.

Management made the following comments:

Some job order personnel were not enrolled in the Biometrics System because they were assigned in clean and green programs and garbage and waste collection and disposal. For this reason, their work usually starts from 5 AM which is beyond the required number of working hours of 8 AM to 5 PM.

Identification cards (IDs) were given to employees however, the printing of the IDs were not finished by the private individual contracted by the Municipality.

PDS and IDs were no longer required from some JO’s because they are personally known to the Municipal Officials.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Engineer:

submit a written explanation on the noted discrepancies in programmed quantity for sand and the inclusion of aggregate base course which was not reflected in the construction plan;

submit the detailed engineering documents in the implementation of the FMR to enable the technical team to validate the accomplishment of work/pay items, if any; and

henceforth, ensure that the required plans and specifications are prepared prior to implementation thereof.
observations.managementAction:
As a response, the Municipal Engineer explained in his letter that the use of aggregate base coarse was necessary and used only for the correction of road base prior to PCCP. The Audit Team was also informed that the volume computation for sand included waste factor. Nonetheless, the Municipal officials submitted a revised construction plan which reflects the aggregate base coarse.

Per Construction Estimate guidelines, with regards to the kind of mixture for concrete pay item, the quantity of sand, among others, is pegged at 0.5 per cu.m. hence, a waste factor is no longer necessary.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Engineer strictly comply with DPWH DO No. 11, series of 2014 on the design standards for farm to market roads.
observations.managementAction: Management assured strict compliance with the said DPWH regulation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive take necessary actions for the settlement of the audit disallowances.
observations.managementAction: Appeals for both NDs were made by the concerned official on the disallowed salaries, however, COA RO3 Decision No. 2016-034 dated June 27, 2016 was issued affirming ND No. 2014-001-(13) on the basis that “the Budget Circular made a general mention of “government personnel” without explicitly making a decision between government officials and employees of the National Government and LGUs. Neither did it mention that the term is applicable only to certain employees nor that LGU employees are exempt from the said provision. Applying the rule on statutory construction “ubi ex non distinguit, nec nos distinguere” (when the law does not distinguish, we should not distinguish), the officials and employees of LGUs are likewise subject to the limitation that the one-time grant of PEI for year 2013 shall be at the rate not exceeding ₱5,000.00 per employee.

observations.recommendation: We recommend to the Management to direct the Municipal Treasurer to monitor closely the remittances of the collecting officers as a deterrent to possible losses or any eventuality that may lead to misstatements of cash-local treasury and income accounts.  Likewise, ensure prompt remittance of amount collected in order to account what rightfully belongs to the government and to institute the necessary appropriate action, if warranted, by the circumstances.

We recommend to the Management to direct the Municipal Treasurer to exercise the diligence of a good father of a family in monitoring the remittances of collections of all revenue collectors to preclude misappropriation of the meager resources of the government, otherwise he shall be jointly and solidarily liable with them for any loss of government funds under his control.

We recommend to the Local Chief Executive to follow the provisions stated in Joint Circular No. 2015-001 dated February 8, 2015 on the Salient Features and Guidelines on the Confidential Fund.

Likewise, we directed the Municipal Accountant and the Budget Officer to advise the Local Chief Executive on the documentary requirements needed in the granting of additional cash advances for Confidential Fund.

We recommend to the Management to require the Municipal Accountant to submit all DVs to the Audit Team within the reglementary period provided under COA Circular No. 95-006 dated May 18, 1995 in order to establish the propriety of the affected accounts, otherwise, the same will be suspended in audit.
We recommend to the Management to strictly comply with the above issuances by submitting perfected contracts/purchase orders and inspection reports.

We recommend to the Local Chief Executive to direct those concerned officials and employees to properly allocate expenditures to what is required for the medical care and health benefits of the public.

We recommend to the Management to direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to remit in full the withheld taxes in accordance with the Revenue Memorandum Order No. 8-200 to avoid penalties for delay or non-remittance.

We recommend that Management direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to immediately remit the withheld amount so as not to deprive the government officials and employees of the benefits and privileges due them. The Municipal Treasurer must see to it that remittances of the contributions and withheld premiums and loans to the GSIS, PAG-IBIG and PHILHEALTH shall be given priority over and above the payment of any and all obligations, except salaries and wages of its employees.
We recommend to the Management to adhere strictly to the provisions of the COA Circular No. 2012-002 by submitting the Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF to COA within the legal time frame.
